summary: Gruber reviews the iPad 3, focusing on its retina display as the defining upgrade. He frames Apple's iPad strategy as iterative clarification of an unchanged concept — a 9.7-inch touchscreen with extraordinary battery life — rather than reinvention. The retina display transforms reading and photo viewing into print-quality experiences, while LTE networking delivers speeds perceptually equivalent to Wi-Fi. Apple's engineering priorities are revealed by the tradeoffs: they accepted a slightly thicker, heavier device to preserve battery life while adding LTE and the power-hungry retina display. Gruber notes that the iPad he dreamed of in his 2010 review — double resolution, a gigabyte of RAM — arrived two years later at the same price, not at MacBook Pro prices.
key_points: The retina display is the singular defining upgrade, making text look 'impossibly good' and exposing how low-resolution most web images are
Apple's priorities are clearly legible in the tradeoffs: battery life and display quality won over thinness and weight, while CPU speed was deprioritized in favor of GPU performance
LTE networking delivers Wi-Fi-equivalent speeds with minimal battery impact, which Gruber calls a remarkable engineering accomplishment
The iPad concept has remained fundamentally unchanged across three generations — each iteration is a clarification, not a reinvention
Intel's CPU expertise is becoming less important than graphics processing for personal computing, a structural shift Apple's choices highlight
money_quotes: Reading on the big retina display is pure joy.
The iPad display is so good that it shows, like no device before it, just how crummy most images on the web are.
My hypothetical dream iPad from 2010 is today the just-plain real-life iPad.
With really small pixels like these, anti-aliased text looks impossibly good — and what you thought looked pretty good before now looks blurry.
What is changed — and what is unchanged — in this newest iteration of the iPad reveals Apple's priorities.
Their expertise — CPUs — is no longer the most important processing bottleneck for personal computing. Graphics are.
Better to make the iPad slightly thicker and heavier than have battery life suffer slightly.
The retina display is amazing, everything in the UI feels faster, and the price points remain the same. What's not to love? It's that simple.
key_insight: The iPad 3's retina display is not just an incremental spec bump but a clarification of the iPad's original vision, and Apple's willingness to make the device slightly thicker to preserve battery life reveals that experience always trumps specs in their priority hierarchy.
